title: @majeanson/lac — The CLI
problem: Developers need a terminal-first way to create, search, validate, and manage feature.json files without leaving their workflow.
analysis: The public entry point to the LAC ecosystem — a single npm package (@majeanson/lac) bundling three binaries: `lac` (CLI), `lac-lsp` (language server), and `lac-mcp` (MCP server). 16+ commands cover the full lifecycle; all operate on the local filesystem with no server required.
implementation: Entry point is src/index.ts, a Commander.js router delegating to src/commands/*.ts. The filesystem walker and scanner power all read commands. Config is loaded from .lac/config.json; interactive commands use the `prompts` library. Static site export generates self-contained HTML/CSS from src/templates/. Bundled with tsdown to ESM.

decisions.decision: Bundle all three binaries (CLI, LSP, MCP) into one npm package
decisions.rationale: One `npm install -g @majeanson/lac` gives the developer everything — no separate install steps for server components.
alternativesConsidered: Separate packages per binary — cleaner separation but friction-heavy install

decisions.rationale: Mature, well-documented, and supports nested subcommands cleanly for 16+ commands.
Features live in the repo alongside code and git tracks history — no infra to provision or maintain.
alternativesConsidered: SQLite sidecar — faster queries but severs git-native workflow

knownLimitations: Bundling all binaries means CLI-only users always install LSP and MCP too
Interactive prompts require a real TTY — headless CI needs --yes flags
No concurrent write safety — simultaneous lac fill calls on the same file conflict
successCriteria: `npm install -g @majeanson/lac` succeeds and `lac init` creates a valid .lac/ workspace with all commands running error-free.

lineage.spawnReason: The ecosystem overview needs a dedicated feature per package; the CLI is the primary user-facing entry point.
